label: Acetyl-CoA
summary: Central metabolic precursor derived from glucose catabolism via pyruvate dehydrogenase. Entry point into the mevalonate pathway for isoprenoid biosynthesis in engineered S. cerevisiae. Overexpression of HMGS and tHMGR dramatically increased flux from acetyl-CoA toward the sesquiterpene branch.
citation: Ro et al., 2006. Production of the antimalarial drug precursor artemisinic acid in engineered yeast. Nature 440, 940–943.

label: HMG-CoA
summary: 3-Hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA is the substrate of HMG-CoA reductase (HMGR), the rate-limiting enzyme of the mevalonate pathway. Overexpression of a truncated, constitutively active form of yeast HMGR (tHMGR) was critical to increase carbon flux toward artemisinin precursors by bypassing feedback inhibition by downstream sterols.

label: Mevalonate
summary: Mevalonate is a six-carbon branched hydroxy acid produced by the irreversible NADPH-dependent reduction of HMG-CoA by HMGR. It is the first committed intermediate of the mevalonate (isoprenoid) pathway. Upregulation of HMGR and downstream MVK/PMK via plasmid overexpression dramatically boosted mevalonate production in the Ro et al. S. cerevisiae strain.

label: FPP
summary: Farnesyl pyrophosphate (FPP) is the universal C15 isoprenoid building block and the pivotal branch-point metabolite at which sterol biosynthesis and sesquiterpene synthesis diverge. Ro et al. re-directed FPP away from the essential ERG9 squalene synthase branch by downregulating ERG9 via a MET3 promoter, maximising amorphadiene synthase substrate availability.

label: Amorphadiene
summary: Amorpha-4,11-diene is the first committed sesquiterpene intermediate specific to the artemisinin branch, produced by amorphadiene synthase (ADS) from Artemisia annua. Heterologous expression of ADS in S. cerevisiae by Ro et al. established the dedicated biosynthetic route to artemisinin. ADS catalyses a complex cyclisation of FPP involving a 1,10 ring closure followed by a 1,3 hydride shift.

label: Artemisinic Acid
summary: Artemisinic acid is the direct precursor to artemisinin, produced by a three-step oxidation of amorphadiene catalysed by the cytochrome P450 CYP71AV1 (co-expressed with its reductase CPR) from A. annua. Artemisinic acid accumulates extracellularly in the engineered yeast, enabling simple recovery by solvent extraction, a key design advantage of the Ro et al. pathway.

label: Artemisinin
summary: Artemisinin is a sesquiterpene endoperoxide lactone and the most effective first-line antimalarial for Plasmodium falciparum, reaching over 500 million patients per year. Its unique 1,2,4-trioxane pharmacophore generates reactive oxygen species inside infected red blood cells. The Ro et al. 2006 landmark engineering study produced a scalable microbial supply of its precursor, tackling a critical access problem in global health.
citation: Ro et al., 2006. Nature 440, 940–943. WHO Essential Medicine since 2002.
